## [00:00 - 02:59]

Hey everyone, it's Sam Evans here and welcome to week seven which is called the next level. And in this first module today we're going to be talking about secrets of exponential scale. So how to take your consulting business that you've started or that you've grown in or enhanced throughout the consulting accelerator program. How to really take that business and ratchet it up and scale it up to that next level so that you can start making high six figures, high seven figures, even eight figures with your consulting business. Now this is a topic that really I haven't seen anyone address ever like in the history of consulting. And there's this big myth and this big notion out there that consulting doesn't scale. That's what so many people out there believe because traditionally consulting has been a bunch of dudes like that work for Boston Consulting Group wearing suits and going to meet corporate clients in person, you know, flying there, going into board meetings and things like that and sitting there and actually delivering one-on-one advice manually. And obviously that doesn't scale. You can't take one human consultant doing that and scale them up because there's only so much time in the day and there's only so many different boardrooms or meetings that you can be in at one time, which is one. And so traditionally consulting hasn't scaled and using the old model of consulting, you know, this is really right. Consulting doesn't scale using the old model. And for a long time a lot of people thought, oh, I don't want to start a consulting business. I want to do something online or I want to do e-commerce or some sort of product business or some sort of technology business that can really scale up and get me a high income, something in the high seven figures, eight figures or more. But really no one's looked at an analyzed consulting and really thought about it and thought, how can we scale it? Can we change the model of it? And is there a way to really revolutionize consulting so that we can turn what has been a service into a product and into something that we can really scale with technology and all of that? So throughout week seven, we're going to be talking about how to take your consulting business to that next level and how to really scale it up and ratchet it up. So to start off today in our video, what I want to talk about is, you know, why consulting doesn't scale and why the old model means that consulting business has kind of hit a ceiling and they get stuck at a particular point. And the best way to show you that and the best way to really explain how consulting breaks down at scale is to introduce you to something called the flywheel.

## [03:16 - 06:17]

So something called a flywheel. Now this is a fascinating concept which I learned from Jeff Bezos who founded and still runs Amazon. He's the richest man on planet Earth and at this time Amazon's the most valuable company on planet Earth. So if there's anyone you want to learn from, you know, Jeff Bezos is one of them. So when I read Jeff Bezos' letters to shareholders, he talked about how Amazon has a flywheel and when he explained it, it made total sense and it really showed me and really explained to me how Amazon has grown to be the success that it is today. And then when I started to understand flywheels, I started to notice that really any good business has a flywheel and all of the top businesses that we see today like Airbnb, Amazon, Facebook, Google, Uber, all of these businesses, even Instagram and Facebook, all of these businesses have flywheels and that's why they've grown to be as big as they are. So let's talk about a flywheel and what it is. So if we start off here, you know, a business's main goal generally is to grow. Businesses want to grow bigger, they want to make more money, they want to dominate the market, they want to make more profit. This is really the main objective of most businesses and when it comes to growth, a flywheel looks something like this. I'll just write this out here and then I'll explain it. So this is Amazon's flywheel and this is what Jeff Bezos sketched out and really used to explain how their business works and how it's grown to be the size it is. So with Amazon, in order to grow, they need to get more sellers onto the platform. So with Amazon, they want to get more different businesses to come along and offer their products for sale. So in the beginning, Amazon just sold books but then these days, really, they sell everything and people have started to call Amazon the everything store. So one of Amazon's goals is to get more sellers onto the platform and if they get more sellers onto the platform, that means that there's going to be more selection and that means there's going to be more things available for customers to browse and buy and that's good for customers because as customers, we want access to everything at the best price. And then if there's more selection, that means customers are going to have a better experience.

## [06:17 - 09:23]

If they go to Amazon and search, they're going to be happy and if they buy something on Amazon and there's a huge variety of things which they can buy, they're going to be happy. And if there's a better experience for customers, that means there's going to be more traffic. More people are going to talk about it, more people are going to tell other people about it and then also more customers are going to keep coming back and buying again. So it's going to lead to more traffic and when there's more traffic, there's more customers and when there's more customers, more sellers want to come onto the platform because now that there's tons of customers, more sellers want to be on there so that they can sell to them and this just is a big vicious cycle because for every time they add more here, more happens here, more happens here, more happens here, more happens here. So Amazon really has a flywheel and this is what they call Amazon's flywheel. Now let me explain this for a company like Uber because with Uber, this might be more drivers here. The more drivers Uber gets onto their platform, then the more selection users have when they open up the Uber app. The more drivers are on there, if you pull out the app and you look at it and you see how far away a driver is, it's going to say like one minute or three minutes because there's so many drivers out there. That's good for you because you don't want to wait too long and then if there's more selection, it's a better experience and if people have a better experience with Uber, they're going to use it more often and they're going to tell more people about it, which leads to more traffic, which therefore leads to more customers. So you see this business has the exact same thing, Amazon and Uber. Now we'll use one more example like Airbnb. So the more sellers that they have, that's the more owners of property, putting their properties available for rent on Airbnb, then the more selection there is on Airbnb. The more selection, the better the experience, the better the experience, the more traffic, the more traffic, the more customers, the more customers, the more sellers. So you see how this works. It's a vicious cycle and it's a feedback loop. And every time you add one more here, everything else grows. And this and really only this is the main reason why those companies have grown to be as big as they are today. We've got Uber, Facebook, Airbnb, Google, all of them really. Instagram, they all grow through a feedback loop and a flywheel. Now I want to show you how consulting breaks down when we scale it. And really it's something I call an anti-flywheel. All right, this is up in just a sec.

## [09:23 - 12:25]

So while those companies I just explained to you have a flywheel, which is helping them grow, it's a benefit for them. Most consulting businesses have something the exact opposite, which is something I've called the anti-flywheel. And this is what it looks like. So this can be consulting, coaching or any service business. So here if we have more time and then less client attention, worse results, the worst the results, the less or call this the experience. Worst experience we get, the less customers. Right. So this is what happens with pretty much every single coaching, consulting and service-based business on planet Earth. So it works the opposite way. So the more customers you get with a one-on-one consulting business, then that means the more time you've got to spend helping your customers. And then the more time you've got to spend helping your customers. There's only so much time a human being has. And so the max hours we can really work in a day is probably about 15 and the max days we can work is seven. So we max out at that point. That's all the time we have possible. So once we max out there, and if every new client we bring on is demanding more of our time and more of our attention, well, what's going to happen is our clients are going to start getting less attention. So if we have 10 clients and we work 15 hours a day, seven days a week, which isn't really recommended, but if we have 10 clients and do that, then they're going to be getting a particular amount of time each. But if I go and add another 10 clients, the only thing that can budge there is the amount of time that I'm giving per client. That's the only variable in that equation that can move. So what's going to end up happening, if I double my clients with a one-on-one consulting business, each client is going to get less of my attention. They're going to get less of my care. And when they get less of my attention and less of my care, they're going to get worse

## [12:25 - 15:25]

results. Because if I can only spend half the amount of time on a client's project, then it's really going to get half the results that it usually would. And if they get worse results, then their experience is worse. And when they have bad experiences, well, they're going to quit. They're going to stop paying us. And they're also going to tell other people, don't buy from him either or don't buy from that company either. And when that happens, the whole thing just goes shoo shoo shoo shoo and it just goes down. It goes backwards. And so this is really why most consulting, coaching, or any service-based businesses, they have something called an anti-flywheel, which means that up until a particular point, we're good. Like up until, say, 10 clients or maybe at max 20 clients, we can actually deliver an okay experience to everyone. But past that point, things start to get worse and worse and worse. And the easiest way I can really cement this point for you and really help you understand it is by using a mechanic business as an example. So I'm sure everyone's taken a car to a mechanic before or a garage or a workshop, whatever you call them. And let's say that you take your car to a mechanic and the mechanic's running this business by himself and, or let's say he's got two staff, right? Let's say the total capacity of that mechanic's business is 30 clients or 30 customers a month. That means that the max they can do in a month is 30 jobs. Well, if they were to scale that up to 60 jobs, that means that each job is going to get half the attention that it usually would. And if a mechanic starts giving all of his clients or all of her clients half the attention, then the job's going to be half as good. And when that happens, worse results, clients aren't going to be happy, the experience is going to be bad. That means that they're not going to come back to that mechanic and they're going to tell other people not to go to that mechanic. And so what happens with most consulting, coaching or really any service-based business? What happens is we can scale up into a particular point and then we find kind of like a glass ceiling. And when we hit that, there's nothing we can do to go beyond it. And every effort that we make to try and go beyond that, it actually hurts us because for every additional customer or client we're bringing on, we're actually hurting ourselves and crippling our chances of success. And so what I want to do right now is talk about this glass ceiling that really limits everyone.

## [15:25 - 18:27]

So with a typical consulting business, you might be growing like this. Let's say here we have growth in dollars and here we have time in years. Let's say this is year one, year two, year three, year four, year five. And this is growth here, 100K, 200K. And then up here we have one million. So with a typical consulting business, if it uses the model done for you or the one-on-one consulting model, this is what typically happens. The business starts growing here and at year one, let's say it's making 100K and they've got a total of 10 clients. So you've got 10 clients making 100K and you're one year in. So the consultant thinks, oh, this is simple. I've just got to do double what I'm doing right now. So then we go out to year two here and here, let's just say that is 200K and now we have 20 clients. Then what happens, the consultant thinks, oh, I know, I'll just double it again. I'll go out and try and get 30 clients. And so that would be 40 clients at that point. So the next year they come out here to year three and they try and get 40 clients. And then what happens is the growth completely slows down and they're only making about 300K. So they start to hit this point where they've got massive diminishing returns. They double the amount of clients they've got, but they don't double the amount of money they're making. They only move their income up a tiny bit. And then they might think, I know, we just need more clients. So they go out here and they get 60 clients. And then out here, they might notice that they're only making 300K again. So they're making the same amount of money. So you can see here that something kind of goes wrong. We've got a model where for every 10 clients we get, we get 100K. The next year we double it and it works. We double the amount of clients. We double the amount of income. But then the next year we double it and we only get a little bit more. And then we go and add on 20 more clients and we actually make the same amount of money. So we've gone and grown. We're doing more work. We're delivering more stuff to more people. But we're actually making the same amount of money. And sometimes this can actually happen to the point where you start making less money. So it is possible to actually grow up to say 80 clients here and actually start losing

## [18:27 - 21:28]

money and being back here at like 100K. Now you might be thinking, how is that possible? How can that happen? And it's really all to do with the flywheel because human beings are very complex to manage and they need managers and they need all of that. And also most of the time people spend or most of the time people waste working on client projects is all of the stuff in between the actual work. So meetings, communication, organizing things, busy work like that. And what happens is when you start adding more and more and more people, more possible connections happens between all of those different people and it gets exponentially more complicated and exponentially gets worse. And that's why just growing your consulting business like this, it's not the way to go about it. And really I refer to this here, let's say it's 300 grand a year. This is where I notice a lot of people hit this ceiling. I've seen people hit this ceiling in between 100K and 300K a year. That's where I see most people with the traditional consulting model just kind of hit this glass ceiling and they can't really escape it and they can't make any more. Now this model of growth here, we call this linear growth. So when you have a business, there's really two different types of growth that you can have. One is linear and the other is exponential. So we'll start with number one, linear. And that is kind of a straight line like that. And here we have growth in dollars and here we have time in years. This is a linear model and this is what most, if not all, businesses have. And that means for every, let's say for every amount of, let's say with every 20 clients you make 100 grand, then if you add it to 40, you might make 200, then you might make it 80 and you only make a little bit more. This model of growth here is called linear. And it doesn't matter what you try and do with a linear growth curve. You're going to hit a point where you just can't make any more money even if you keep doing more and more work. And it will reach a point where you actually start to go backwards. The other type is exponential.

## [21:28 - 24:30]

And if we've got the same model here, growth in dollars and then time in years. And this exponential curve, it goes like this. Now here's what's fascinating about the exponential model. So here if you have one year and you've got say 20 clients and you're making 100k, then out here you might have two years and you might be making 200k and you've got, so you went from 20 clients to 40 clients and it doubled. And all of a sudden you come out here, three years and here things have gone exponential. And so the key point with this one is that the more clients you have, it reaches a point where you start to make more money. And with this one here, the more clients you have, you start to make less money. And what's crazy about the exponential model here is that with the linear model, if you have to invest 100 grand in advertising to get out say 200 grand, so you've got a two to one return, spend 100k, make 200k, spend a million, make 2 million. Well what can be crazy about this one is at a particular point you might be making 100 million and you don't have to spend anything on advertising. It has an exact opposite effect. But with this one, if you start spending 10 million, you might not make anything because you've got diminishing returns. Now this has always fascinated me. I always wondered like how does this happen? But how can this one model here curve up like a hockey stick like this? And how does this other model go the opposite way? And this fascinated me for a long time until I realised that it was really the flywheel that was driving this. So behind any exponential curve, there is a feedback loop driving it and the feedback loop driving this one is the flywheel. And basically for a flywheel to exist, if a business wants to have a flywheel, this needs to be true. For every additional customer, the experience must get better.

## [24:30 - 27:33]

So the two variables we've got here is additional customers and better. So if you want to have a flywheel in your business and if you want to have an exponential growth curve in your business, well this must be true. For every additional customer, the experience must get better. So I told you before about consulting, coaching, service-based businesses like the Mechanic shop or like the one-on-one consultant or the done-for-you consultant, they have for every additional customer, the experience actually gets worse because for every additional customer they have, they can spend less time with them and the less time they spend with them, the worse the results are. So that is why most done-for-you consulting, one-on-one consulting or one-on-one coaching businesses, they have an anti-flywheel because for every additional customer it gets worse. But companies like Uber, Airbnb, Amazon and all of that, for every additional customer the experience actually gets better because the more customers are on Amazon, the more sellers are on Amazon, the more sellers are on Amazon, the more stuff there is available, the more stuff there is available, the more customers there are and so it goes up like that and it has a massive feedback loop. And so really with consulting, the way most people do it, the done-for-you model or the one-on-one model, it doesn't scale because for every additional customer the experience actually gets worse. And so that is why the glass ceiling happens and that is why most consultants run into this glass ceiling and they really get stuck there and they get suffocated there. So you might be thinking, I understand now Sam, I understand that to grow a business exponentially and to grow a business that is going to be big, scale well and really produce a lot of money and a lot of profit, I need to have a flywheel. And to have a flywheel, I know that for every one additional customer I have, the experience must get better. So we understand what is going on there. Now how do we actually get a flywheel in our business? If you have got a consulting business right now that is using the done-for-you model or the one-on-one model or the one-on-one coaching model, how do you actually make it better for every new customer? Well you will remember from, I am pretty sure, yeah week one, module three I am pretty sure, that it is called the evolution of a consultant.

## [27:33 - 30:36]

So I am sure a lot of you can remember this and back in week one of the accelerator program, I explained how the journey of most consultants goes. And there is nothing wrong with that because pretty much every consultant starts doing that. I started by being a generalist consultant and a lot of others do and there is nothing wrong with that. And the max you can really make with this model here is about 100k a year. Then you can evolve from here and from here you have a niche done for you consultant. And at this point here you can scale up to maybe 300k per year. And this can be, you have gone from generalist to a niche so this is when you start becoming a specialist in one particular thing and you start helping one particular type of business. And then from there you evolve into niche one on one consulting. And this is where you get a bit better at what you are doing. So here you are actually doing all the work for everyone and you are actually the person delivering the work, you might be using contractors, you might have an agency model or you might be doing the work yourself. You are basically you are on the tools and you are the one doing the manual labour or you have got a team doing the manual labour. Here the next stage of evolution you are still in the same niche but you are now just advising. So you are working with the same types of clients but you are telling them what to do and then they are taking your advice and your words away and then they are doing the work themselves or they are hiring other people to do the work. Now to graduate to this model here you pretty much just need to get better at the done for you stuff to the point that your advice now has inherent value. So when you are down here your advice does not have that much value because you are not that much of an expert. So the only way that you can provide value is by doing the work but up here now your advice has value on its own. And the max you can really make using this model it is in between 300K and at max really

## [30:36 - 33:41]

500K but you know that is really stretching it because you are severely limited here by your time because you can only do one on one work which means you have only got so many hours in the day you have only got so you know you are bound by your human limitations. Then the next way to scale up from here is to go into group coaching. So if you go into group coaching here now instead of working one on one you have now combined multiple people into a group and you are doing one to many. So now you have leveraged yourself because instead of running on the economic model of one to one now you have got one to 20. So usually in the amount of time it would take for you to consult one person you can now consult 20 people. And when you start doing this your income jumps big time. Here you can start making about $1 million a year with the leveraged group coaching model and then from there you can go into online courses and training. And with online courses and training it really just skyrockets exponentially because what you do with this model is instead of teaching it one on one instead of doing the work or instead of teaching 20 people at a time you create videos and PDFs and training materials that can work for you forever with an infinite amount of people. Because if you invest time into one video one time then a million people can watch that video and get consulted themselves. So most people haven't really figured this out. They think that doing online courses isn't doing consulting but that's so backwards because online courses are consulting people. It's just not a human consulting people. It's a video consulting people. But the human made that video. So really it still is consulting. It's just a different method of service delivery. It's a different way to bring the change and the transformation to the actual client. But you're still consulting. And with this model here you can really go 10 million to 100 million and more. results. A lot of people they're afraid, they think I need to do the work for them. I need to do the 101 stuff because people like the 101 or they like the done for you. Well when you've got multiple people in a Facebook group or things like that the different people within the Facebook group can communicate with each other. So that means for every one new client you've got there's more people in the Facebook group. The more people you've got in the Facebook group the faster people's questions are going to get answered, the more potential help someone's going to get when they ask a question. And so we start to have feedback loop types of things going on in this sort of model. But with the one on one model we never have a feedback loop, we never have a flywheel present because all the sweat, blood and tears that you pour into one client none of the other clients get to see that.
